metadata.summary: Lightweight monitoring and logging for solo VPS platforms using journald, health-check scripts, and Telegram alerts without Prometheus/Grafana.

content.title: Monitoring and Logging
content.summary: Lightweight monitoring and logging for solo developer VPS platforms using journald, health-check scripts, and Telegram alerts without Prometheus/Grafana. Provides journalctl query patterns, log rotation config, FLOW-style autonomous hourly/daily reports, and status dashboard.

why.p: A solo developer server needs 95% of enterprise monitoring value at 1% of the complexity. Journald + a few shell scripts + Telegram alerts cover service-down detection, disk/memory thresholds, OOM kills, and daily status summaries without running a full observability stack.

list.item: Setting up observability from scratch on a solo-developer VPS
A service went down silently and you need alerting before it happens again
Writing a health-check script for a cron job or FLOW guardian
Building an autonomous monitoring loop that sends Telegram alerts on anomalies
Investigating a production incident retroactively via journalctl
Multi-server environments where Prometheus/Grafana/Loki is justified (use pro/infra/devops-engineer)
Kubernetes clusters (use Datadog, Grafana Loki, or similar)
Compliance-heavy environments requiring tamper-proof audit-trail log storage
High-frequency trading or sub-second SLOs where journald latency is unacceptable

rule.statement: Use --user flag for systemd user services; omit it only for system units like nginx or docker.
rule.rationale: User services run under loginctl linger sessions and their logs are scoped separately. Using system-level journalctl silently returns no output for user services.

rule.statement: Prefer -p err over grepping for "ERROR" strings; journald priority filtering is structured and faster.
rule.rationale: Application logs may write "error" as a string without setting priority. -p err captures systemd-level failures like OOM kills and service crashes regardless of application log format.

example.code:
# Follow a user service in real-time journalctl --user -u nero-core -f
# Last 50 lines of a user service journalctl --user -u nero-core -n 50
# Errors from last 2 hours across all nero services journalctl --user -u 'nero-*' -p err --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager
# Kernel OOM kills journalctl -k --since "1 hour ago" | grep -i "out of memory"
# Service restart events journalctl --user -u nero-core | grep -E "(Started|Stopped|Failed)"
# JSON output for scripted analysis journalctl --user -u nero-core -o json --since "1 hour ago"
# Count errors per service in last 24h for svc in nero-core nero-channel-web nero-channel-tg; do count=$(journalctl --user -u "$svc" -p err --since "24 hours ago" --no-pager 2>/dev/null | wc -l) echo "$svc: $count errors" done

# Wrong: omits --user, returns nothing for user services journalctl -u nero-core -f
# Wrong: grep on raw text is fragile and slow journalctl --user -u nero-core | grep -i error
# Wrong: no --no-pager in scripts causes blocking journalctl --user -u nero-core | wc -l
example.note: Always add --no-pager in scripts. Use -p for priority filtering, not grep on message text.

list.item: -u — filter by unit name; supports glob patterns like 'nero-*'
--user — scope to user-session units (loginctl linger must be enabled)
-p err — priority filter: emerg, alert, crit, err, warning, notice, info, debug
--since / --until — time window; accepts "2 hours ago", "today", ISO timestamps
-f — follow (tail -f equivalent)
-n N — last N lines
-o format — output format: short, short-iso, json, cat (message only)
-b / -b -1 — current boot / previous boot
-k — kernel messages (dmesg equivalent via journald)
--disk-usage — show total journal storage consumed
--no-pager — never paginate; required in all scripts

rule.statement: Set Storage=persistent so logs survive reboots; default volatile storage loses all history on each boot.
rule.rationale: Incident investigation almost always needs logs from the previous boot cycle. Volatile storage makes post-reboot debugging impossible.

rule.statement: Cap SystemMaxUse at 10% of total disk; for a 100G VPS, 2G is a reasonable ceiling.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, a single chatty service can fill the disk. journald enforces the cap automatically, rotating oldest logs first.

rule.statement: Always configure Docker daemon to rotate container logs before they consume unbounded disk space.
rule.rationale: Docker's default log driver (json-file) has no rotation. A single high-volume container can fill the disk silently.

example.note: Place at /etc/docker/daemon.json. Restart Docker after changes: sudo systemctl restart docker. Existing containers use old settings until recreated.

antipattern.description: Starting containers without log rotation configured.
antipattern.reason: A container logging 1MB/minute fills a 100G disk in 70 days. With no rotation, the only fix is manual cleanup under incident pressure.

rule.statement: Use delaycompress with compress so the currently active log file is never compressed while it may still be written to.
rule.rationale: Compressing an open file corrupts it. delaycompress ensures only the previous rotation's file gets compressed.

rule.statement: A health-check script must cover all four layers: infrastructure (Docker), application (systemd), endpoints (HTTP), and system resources.
rule.rationale: A service can be "active" in systemd but returning 500s; an endpoint check catches what systemd status misses. Resource checks catch slow degradation before hard failures.

rule.statement: Use exit codes: 0 = healthy, 1 = warnings, 2 = errors. Never exit 0 when checks fail.
rule.rationale: Cron jobs and monitoring wrappers test exit codes to decide whether to send alerts. A silent non-zero exit from a health check defeats its purpose.

rule.statement: Print OK/WARN/FAIL prefix on every check line so output is machine-parseable without regex.
rule.rationale: Downstream scripts and log scanners can grep for FAIL without parsing natural language sentences.

list.item: Infrastructure: Docker daemon active, each container running (postgres, redis, rabbitmq)
Application: each systemd user service active (nero-core, channel-web, channel-tg, web, beat)
Endpoints: HTTP health endpoint returns 2xx, frontend returns 2xx
Resources: disk percent, memory percent, CPU load vs. core count, swap usage
Disk: warn above 80%, fail above 90%
Memory: warn above 85%, fail above 95%
CPU load: warn when load/cores > 90%
Swap: warn when more than 50% of swap is used
HTTP endpoint timeout: 5 seconds max

example.code:
# Wrong: always exits 0, alert wrappers never trigger ./health-check.sh || true
# Wrong: no resource checks, disk fills silently check_service "Docker" "systemctl is-active docker" echo "Done" exit 0
example.note: Health checks that always exit 0 are worse than no health check — they create false confidence.

rule.statement: Run health checks with an alert-on-failure wrapper in cron, not by piping directly to Telegram inside the check script.
rule.rationale: Separating health-check logic from alerting logic lets you run the same check manually without triggering alerts.

example.code:
# Run health check every 5 minutes; alert if non-zero exit */5 * * * * /usr/local/bin/health-check.sh || /usr/local/bin/notify-telegram.sh "Health check FAILED on $(hostname)"
# FLOW hourly monitor (alert-only, no noise on success) 0 * * * * /home/nero/workspace/scripts/flow-hourly.sh >> /var/log/nero-flow.log 2>&1

rule.statement: Store TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID in ~/.env (chmod 600), never hardcoded in scripts.
rule.rationale: Scripts in ~/bin or /usr/local/bin may be world-readable. Credentials in .env are loaded at runtime and never committed to version control.

rule.statement: Make the notify function accept both a positional argument and stdin so it composes with pipes.
rule.rationale: Pipeline composition (command | notify-telegram.sh) is the most common usage pattern in monitoring scripts. Argument form is convenient for one-liners.

rule.statement: Truncate messages to 4000 characters before sending; Telegram rejects messages over 4096 chars.
rule.rationale: Long error output from a failing script will silently fail to deliver if not truncated, defeating the alerting purpose.

rule.statement: Wrap any critical script in an alert-on-failure pattern: run the command, alert only on non-zero exit.
rule.rationale: This keeps alerting logic out of individual scripts and makes alert behavior composable via cron.

rule.statement: FLOW hourly checks must be silent on success; only send Telegram alerts when issues are detected.
rule.rationale: An hourly "all OK" message would send 24 Telegram messages per day per server. Alert fatigue destroys the value of monitoring.

rule.statement: FLOW daily reports send unconditionally at a fixed time regardless of issue state.
rule.rationale: The daily report provides baseline visibility: uptime, resource trends, error count, backup status. It confirms monitoring is working even on quiet days.

list.item: Hourly (0 * * * *): silent health check, alert only on problems
Daily 22:00 (0 22 * * *): full report with uptime, resources, errors, backup status
Weekly Mon 7:00 (0 7 * * 1): trends, capacity, weekly error count
@reboot: startup heartbeat after 30s service stabilization

example.code:
# Hourly health check (alert only on problems) 0 * * * * /home/nero/workspace/scripts/flow-hourly.sh >> /var/log/nero-flow.log 2>&1
# Daily report (22:00) 0 22 * * * /home/nero/workspace/scripts/flow-daily.sh >> /var/log/nero-flow.log 2>&1
# Startup heartbeat (boot + 35s for service stabilization) @reboot sleep 35 && /home/nero/workspace/scripts/startup-heartbeat.sh >> /var/log/nero-startup.log 2>&1

rule.statement: Every alert must include: hostname, timestamp, what failed, severity. Daily reports include uptime and backup status.
rule.rationale: Without hostname, alerts from multiple servers are ambiguous. Without timestamp, alert delivery delay is invisible. Without what-failed, triage requires SSH before understanding scope.

example.code:
FLOW ALERT nero 13:00: - nero-core is DOWN - API health check failed
Daily Report: 2026-03-21 22:00 Host: nero Uptime: up 15 days, 3 hours Services: nero-core active (847MB), nero-channel-web active (185MB) Resources: CPU 1.2 load | RAM 8.5G/30G | Disk 29% Errors (24h): 3 Backup: 20260321_030000 (285MB, 19h ago)

antipattern.description: Sending raw script output or stack traces as Telegram alerts.
antipattern.reason: Telegram messages render poorly for long text. Raw stack traces exceed the 4096-char limit and get dropped silently. Summarize failures, attach details to logs.

template.purpose: Comprehensive health-check script for Docker + systemd + resources
Minimal Telegram send function, stdin or arg mode
FLOW daily report with uptime, resources, errors, backup status
journald.conf with size caps, persistence, rate limiting
logrotate config for application log files
